nutrient_source.title: Beef, rib eye steak/roast, boneless, lip-on, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8" fat, all grades, cooked, grilled — USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central — Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for grilled beef ribeye (boneless, lip-on, lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled). Grass-fed ribeye has marginally higher omega-3 and CLA content than grain-fed; the values here represent a grain-fed/mixed reference and are noted as applicable to grass-fed with minor variation. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown was not separately reported in the primary source entry used.

gi_source.title: N/A — Beef ribeye steak contains negligible carbohydrates (0g per 100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (consulted; no GI value applicable for zero-carbohydrate foods)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled beef ribeye steak contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g. GI is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 200g serving = 0.0. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI value is assigned to pure protein/fat foods.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat content (12.1g/100g; 24.2g per 200g serving) raises LDL cholesterol and worsens cardiovascular risk; sodium is naturally low (65mg/100g = 130mg per 200g serving before any sauce).
hypertension.guidance: Sodium load is low and not a primary concern for hypertension when served without sauce; however, the very high saturated fat content (24.2g per 200g serving, exceeding the 20g daily limit in a single meal) elevates LDL and increases cardiovascular risk — limit portion size and frequency for patients with hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycaemic impact (0g carbohydrate, GL = 0) with high protein (48.2g per 200g serving) supporting satiety and glycaemic stability; however, high saturated fat (24.2g per 200g) contributes to insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk in Type 2 Diabetes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled ribeye steak has zero glycaemic impact and high protein content beneficial for blood glucose stability, but the high saturated fat content (>100% of daily limit per 200g serving) contributes to insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk in Type 2 Diabetes — consume occasionally and pair with non-starchy vegetables; avoid added sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (12.1g/100g; 24.2g per 200g serving — exceeds the 20g daily saturated fat limit in a single serving) and trans fat (0.5g/100g; 1.0g per 200g) raise LDL cholesterol and increase atherosclerotic cardiovascular risk; red meat consumption is associated with increased CVD incidence in prospective cohort studies.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled ribeye steak is high concern for cardiovascular disease due to its very high saturated fat content (24.2g per 200g serving, exceeding the daily limit) and presence of trans fat; patients with established CVD or high cardiovascular risk should limit red meat to occasional consumption, choose leaner cuts (sirloin, tenderloin), and avoid added butter or cream sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Excellent source of zinc (5.6mg/100g = 11.2mg per 200g, exceeding daily RDA), haem iron (2.6mg/100g = 5.2mg per 200g, high bioavailability), and vitamin B12 (2.8mcg/100g = 5.6mcg per 200g, 233% daily RDA); however, high saturated fat (24.2g per 200g) is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ribeye steak provides clinically meaningful zinc, haem iron, and B12 relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management, but the high saturated fat content is pro-inflammatory — limit to occasional consumption and prefer grass-fed sources for marginally higher omega-3 and CLA content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef ribeye steak (grilled, 200g serving, no sauce) is a premium cut of red meat taken from the rib section (ribs 6–12) of beef cattle, characterised by its rich marbling of intramuscular fat that gives it exceptional flavour and tenderness. When grilled, the exterior develops a slightly charred crust while the interior remains juicy, with a deeply savoury, umami-rich taste and a tender, slightly chewy texture. In Singapore, ribeye steak is a staple of Western cafés and steakhouses, sourced from American (USDA-certified), Australian (grass-fed), New Zealand, or Japanese (Wagyu) cattle. Nutritionally, a 200g grilled serving delivers 48.2g protein, 55.6g total fat (24.2g saturated), 0g carbohydrate, 11.2mg zinc (exceeds daily RDA), 5.6mcg vitamin B12 (233% daily RDA), 5.2mg haem iron (high bioavailability), and 60mcg selenium — all clinically meaningful micronutrient contributions. Energy per 200g serving is approximately 696 kcal. The saturated fat content (24.2g per 200g serving, exceeding the 20g daily limit in a single meal) is the primary nutritional concern for cardiovascular disease, Type 2 Diabetes, and hypertension management. Sodium is naturally low (130mg per 200g before sauce). Grass-fed ribeye provides marginally higher conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and omega-3 fatty acids than grain-fed, a genuine but modest benefit. Glycaemic index is not applicable (0g carbohydrate); glycaemic load = 0. Not inherently halal — beef must be halal-certified for Muslim patients. Suitable for gluten-free and dairy-free diets.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for grilled beef ribeye steak (boneless, lip-on, lean and fat, all grades, cooked, grilled) — an approved primary source with high data quality. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-referenced against known nutritional profiles for this cut. Confidence is high.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — beef ribeye steak is a pure protein and fat food with no carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a meat product with no plant fibre. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae and vitamin_c_mg are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked beef ribeye. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) not separately reported in the primary USDA Foundation Foods entry used for this record; detailed fatty acid data available in USDA SR Legacy for similar cuts but not reconciled here. Water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source entry used. Trans_fat_g (0.5g/100g) represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (vaccenic acid, CLA precursors) present in beef fat — not industrially produced trans fats. Grass-fed ribeye note: grass-fed beef has marginally higher CLA and omega-3 content than grain-fed; the values here represent a grain-fed/mixed reference baseline applicable to grass-fed with minor variation. Halal status: beef ribeye is not inherently halal — halal certification depends on slaughter method and supply chain; not marked halal in dietary flags. The occasion_tag 'breakfast' from the input record has been corrected to reflect the actual meal context (dinner/special occasion) as ribeye steak is not a breakfast food in any standard culinary context. Country_of_origin and region fields in the input contained descriptive text rather than proper values — corrected in this output. Tamil local name (மாட்டிறைச்சி ரிப்பை ஸ்டீக் (வறுத்தது)) and Hindi local name (बीफ रिबआई स्टेक (ग्रिल्ड)) added to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singapore food context. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g per 100g); set to null per critical GI rule.
